quizQuestion: The combining form cardi/o refers to which organ?
explanation: Cardi/o comes from Greek 'kardia' meaning heart. Found in cardiology, tachycardia, myocardium, and cardiomegaly.
wowFactor: Every 'cardio' workout is named after the Greek word for heart — kardia!

variants.question: A cardiologist specializes in which organ?

visualDescription: CARDI/O connecting to anatomical heart with arrow

quizQuestion: The combining form hepat/o refers to which organ?
explanation: Hepat/o comes from Greek 'hepar' meaning liver. Found in hepatitis (liver inflammation), hepatomegaly (enlarged liver), and hepatocyte (liver cell).
wowFactor: The liver is the only internal organ that can fully regenerate — hepat/o from Greek 'hepar' captures this powerful organ!

variants.question: Hepatitis is inflammation of which organ?

visualDescription: HEPAT/O connecting to anatomical liver illustration

quizQuestion: The combining form nephr/o refers to which organ?
explanation: Nephr/o comes from Greek 'nephros' meaning kidney. Found in nephrology, nephritis (kidney inflammation), and nephrectomy (surgical removal of a kidney).
wowFactor: Your kidneys filter about 200 liters of blood every day — nephr/o from Greek 'nephros' names this tireless filter!

variants.question: Nephritis is inflammation of which organ?

visualDescription: NEPHR/O connecting to anatomical kidney cross-section

quizQuestion: The combining form encephal/o refers to which organ?
explanation: Encephal/o comes from Greek 'enkephalos' meaning brain. Found in encephalitis (brain inflammation), encephalopathy (brain disease), and electroencephalogram (EEG).
wowFactor: An electroencephalogram (EEG) records your brain's electrical activity — encephal/o literally means 'in the head' in Greek!

variants.question: Encephalitis is inflammation of which organ?

visualDescription: ENCEPHAL/O connecting to anatomical brain illustration

quizQuestion: The combining form pulmon/o refers to which organ?
explanation: Pulmon/o comes from Latin 'pulmo' meaning lung. Found in pulmonology, pulmonary artery (lung artery), and pulmonary embolism (blood clot in the lung).
wowFactor: Pulmonary embolisms are blood clots in the lungs — pulmon/o from Latin 'pulmo' is why lung diseases are called pulmonary!

variants.question: A pulmonologist specializes in which organ?

visualDescription: PULMON/O connecting to anatomical lung pair illustration

quizQuestion: The combining form gastr/o refers to which organ?
explanation: Gastr/o comes from Greek 'gaster' meaning stomach. Found in gastritis (stomach inflammation), gastroenterology, and gastroparesis (stomach paralysis).
wowFactor: Gastronomy — the art of fine dining — shares its root with gastritis: gastr/o, the Greek word for stomach!

variants.question: Gastritis is inflammation of which organ?

visualDescription: GASTR/O connecting to anatomical stomach illustration

quizQuestion: The combining form oste/o refers to which organ?
explanation: Oste/o comes from Greek 'osteon' meaning bone. Found in osteoporosis (porous bone), osteomyelitis (bone infection), and osteoarthritis (joint degeneration).
wowFactor: Osteoporosis literally means 'porous bone' — oste/o from Greek 'osteon' is why bone doctors are called orthopedists!

variants.question: Osteoporosis is a disease affecting which tissue?

visualDescription: OSTE/O connecting to cross-section of bone showing trabecular structure

quizQuestion: The combining form arthr/o refers to which organ?
explanation: Arthr/o comes from Greek 'arthron' meaning joint. Found in arthritis (joint inflammation), arthroscopy (joint camera inspection), and arthroplasty (joint replacement).
wowFactor: Arthroscopic surgery uses a tiny camera inserted into your joint — arthr/o from Greek 'arthron' means 'joint'!

variants.question: Arthritis is inflammation of which structure?

visualDescription: ARTHR/O connecting to anatomical knee joint cross-section

quizQuestion: The combining form my/o refers to which organ?
explanation: My/o comes from Greek 'mys' meaning muscle. Found in myocardium (heart muscle), myalgia (muscle pain), myopathy (muscle disease), and myositis (muscle inflammation).
wowFactor: The myocardium is literally 'heart muscle' — my/o (muscle) + cardi/o (heart) combined into one word!

variants.question: Myalgia is pain in which tissue?

visualDescription: MY/O connecting to anatomical muscle fiber cross-section

quizQuestion: The combining form neur/o refers to which organ?
explanation: Neur/o comes from Greek 'neuron' meaning nerve or sinew. Found in neurology, neuritis (nerve inflammation), neuralgia (nerve pain), and neuropathy (nerve disease).
wowFactor: The word 'neuron' comes from the same Greek root — neur/o — and refers to the individual nerve cells that make up your nervous system!

variants.question: Neuritis is inflammation of which structure?

visualDescription: NEUR/O connecting to anatomical nerve fiber with axon illustration

quizQuestion: The combining form derm/o refers to which organ?
explanation: Derm/o comes from Greek 'derma' meaning skin. Found in dermatology, dermatitis (skin inflammation), dermis (inner skin layer), and epidermis (outer skin layer).
wowFactor: Your skin is actually your largest organ by surface area — derm/o from Greek 'derma' gives us dermatology!

variants.question: Dermatitis is inflammation of which organ?

visualDescription: DERM/O connecting to skin layer cross-section diagram

quizQuestion: The combining form ophthalm/o refers to which organ?
explanation: Ophthalm/o comes from Greek 'ophthalmos' meaning eye. Found in ophthalmology (eye study), ophthalmoscope (eye examination tool), and ophthalmia (eye inflammation).
wowFactor: Ophthalmoscopy lets doctors look through your pupil to see your retina directly — ophthalm/o from Greek 'ophthalmos' means eye!

variants.question: An ophthalmologist specializes in which organ?

visualDescription: OPHTHALM/O connecting to anatomical eye cross-section

quizQuestion: The combining form ot/o refers to which organ?
explanation: Ot/o comes from Greek 'ous/otos' meaning ear. Found in otology (ear study), otitis (ear inflammation), and otoscope (ear examination device).
wowFactor: Otitis media is the medical term for a middle ear infection — ot/o from Greek 'otos' simply means ear!

variants.question: Otitis is inflammation of which organ?

visualDescription: OT/O connecting to anatomical ear cross-section showing cochlea

quizQuestion: The combining form rhin/o refers to which organ?
explanation: Rhin/o comes from Greek 'rhis/rhinos' meaning nose. Found in rhinitis (nasal inflammation), rhinoplasty (nose reshaping surgery), and rhinovirus (common cold virus).
wowFactor: Rhinoplasty — a nose job — literally means 'nose shaping' surgery: rhin/o (nose) + plasty (shaping)!

variants.question: Rhinitis is inflammation of which organ?

visualDescription: RHIN/O connecting to anatomical nose and nasal cavity illustration

quizQuestion: The combining form stomat/o refers to which organ?
explanation: Stomat/o comes from Greek 'stoma/stomatos' meaning mouth or opening. Found in stomatitis (mouth inflammation), stomatology (study of the mouth), and stoma (a surgically created opening).
wowFactor: A stoma is a surgically created opening — stomat/o from Greek 'stoma' means any mouth or opening, not just yours!

variants.question: Stomatitis is inflammation of which structure?

visualDescription: STOMAT/O connecting to open mouth anatomy illustration

quizQuestion: The combining form gloss/o refers to which organ?
explanation: Gloss/o comes from Greek 'glossa' meaning tongue. Found in glossitis (tongue inflammation), glossopharyngeal nerve (tongue-throat nerve), and hypoglossal nerve (under-tongue nerve).
wowFactor: The hypoglossal nerve controls tongue movement — hypo (under) + gloss/o (tongue) in one anatomical name!

variants.question: Glossitis is inflammation of which organ?

visualDescription: GLOSS/O connecting to anatomical tongue illustration

quizQuestion: The combining form dent/o refers to which organ?
explanation: Dent/o comes from Latin 'dens/dentis' meaning tooth. Found in dentistry, dentist, dental, dentures, and orthodontics (straight teeth).
wowFactor: The word 'dandelion' literally means 'lion's tooth' in French (dent-de-lion) — the same Latin root dent/o for tooth!
visualDescription: DENT/O connecting to anatomical tooth cross-section showing enamel and pulp

quizQuestion: The combining form pharyng/o refers to which organ?
explanation: Pharyng/o comes from Greek 'pharynx' meaning throat. Found in pharyngitis (throat inflammation), pharyngoscopy (throat examination), and nasopharynx (nasal throat region).
wowFactor: Pharyngitis is the medical term for a sore throat — pharyng/o from Greek 'pharynx' means the throat passage!

variants.question: Pharyngitis is inflammation of which structure?

visualDescription: PHARYNG/O connecting to throat anatomy showing pharynx location

quizQuestion: The combining form laryng/o refers to which organ?
explanation: Laryng/o comes from Greek 'larynx' meaning voice box. Found in laryngitis (voice box inflammation), laryngoscopy (voice box examination), and laryngectomy (voice box removal).
statement: Laryng/o refers to the larynx (voice box).
wowFactor: Laryngitis makes you lose your voice because it inflames the vocal cords inside the larynx — laryng/o means 'voice box'!

variants.question: Laryngitis is inflammation of which structure?

visualDescription: LARYNG/O connecting to anatomical larynx illustration with vocal cords visible

quizQuestion: The combining form trache/o refers to which organ?
explanation: Trache/o comes from Greek 'tracheia' meaning rough (referring to the rough rings of cartilage). Found in tracheotomy (cutting into the trachea), tracheitis (trachea inflammation), and tracheostomy.
wowFactor: A tracheotomy is an emergency cut into the windpipe to restore breathing — trache/o from Greek 'tracheia' means 'rough-surfaced' for its cartilage rings!

variants.question: A tracheotomy is a surgical opening into which structure?

visualDescription: TRACHE/O connecting to trachea illustration showing cartilage rings

quizQuestion: The combining form bronch/o refers to which organ?
explanation: Bronch/o comes from Greek 'bronchos' meaning windpipe or bronchus. Found in bronchitis (bronchus inflammation), bronchoscopy (bronchus examination), and bronchodilator (airway widening drug).
wowFactor: Bronchodilators like asthma inhalers work by widening the bronchi — bronch/o from Greek 'bronchos' means 'windpipe branch'!

variants.question: Bronchitis is inflammation of which structure?

visualDescription: BRONCH/O connecting to bronchial tree branching from trachea

quizQuestion: The combining form esophag/o refers to which organ?
explanation: Esophag/o comes from Greek 'oisophagos' meaning gullet or food carrier. Found in esophagitis (esophagus inflammation), esophagoscopy, and esophageal cancer.
wowFactor: GERD (acid reflux) is damage to the esophagus from stomach acid — esophag/o from Greek means 'what carries food'!

variants.question: Esophagitis is inflammation of which structure?

visualDescription: ESOPHAG/O connecting to esophagus illustration between throat and stomach

quizQuestion: The combining form enter/o refers to which organ?
explanation: Enter/o comes from Greek 'enteron' meaning intestine. Found in enteritis (intestine inflammation), gastroenterology, parenteral (outside the gut), and enterococcus bacteria.
wowFactor: Parenteral nutrition (IV feeding) literally means 'bypassing the intestine' — enter/o from Greek 'enteron' means gut!

variants.question: Enteritis is inflammation of which organ?

visualDescription: ENTER/O connecting to small intestine coils illustration

quizQuestion: The combining form col/o refers to which organ?
explanation: Col/o comes from Greek 'kolon' meaning the large intestine. Found in colitis (colon inflammation), colonoscopy (colon camera examination), and colorectal cancer.
wowFactor: A colonoscopy uses a camera threaded through the entire colon — col/o from Greek 'kolon' is the large intestine!

variants.question: Colitis is inflammation of which organ?

visualDescription: COL/O connecting to large intestine/colon anatomy illustration

quizQuestion: The combining form rect/o refers to which organ?
explanation: Rect/o comes from Latin 'rectus' meaning straight (the rectum is the straight terminal section of the large intestine). Found in rectal, rectoscopy, and colorectal.
wowFactor: The rectum gets its name from Latin 'rectus' (straight) — rect/o because it is the straightest portion of the digestive tract!

variants.question: Colorectal surgery involves the colon and which other organ?

visualDescription: RECT/O connecting to rectum at end of digestive tract illustration

quizQuestion: The combining form appendic/o refers to which organ?
explanation: Appendic/o comes from Latin 'appendix' meaning appendage or attachment. Found in appendicitis (appendix inflammation), appendectomy (appendix removal), and appendicular.
wowFactor: Appendicitis is one of the most common surgical emergencies — appendic/o from Latin 'appendix' means 'little attachment' hanging from the colon!

variants.question: Appendicitis is inflammation of which organ?

visualDescription: APPENDIC/O connecting to appendix location at junction of small and large intestine

quizQuestion: The combining form cholecyst/o refers to which organ?
explanation: Cholecyst/o comes from Greek 'chole' (bile) + 'kystis' (bladder). Found in cholecystitis (gallbladder inflammation), cholecystectomy (gallbladder removal), and cholecystography.
wowFactor: Cholecystectomy — gallbladder removal — is one of the most common surgeries: chole (bile) + cyst/o (bladder) + ectomy (removal)!

variants.question: Cholecystitis is inflammation of which organ?

visualDescription: CHOLECYST/O connecting to gallbladder illustration beneath the liver

quizQuestion: The combining form pancreat/o refers to which organ?
explanation: Pancreat/o comes from Greek 'pankreas' meaning all flesh (pan = all, kreas = flesh). Found in pancreatitis (pancreas inflammation), pancreatectomy (pancreas removal), and pancreatic cancer.
wowFactor: The pancreas does double duty: it makes digestive enzymes AND insulin — pancreat/o from Greek means 'all flesh' for its meaty appearance!

variants.question: Pancreatitis is inflammation of which organ?

visualDescription: PANCREAT/O connecting to pancreas illustration behind the stomach

quizQuestion: The combining form splen/o refers to which organ?
explanation: Splen/o comes from Greek 'splen' meaning spleen. Found in splenomegaly (enlarged spleen), splenectomy (spleen removal), and splenitis (spleen inflammation).
wowFactor: A ruptured spleen is a life-threatening emergency — splen/o from Greek 'splen' names this blood-filtering organ tucked under your left ribs!

variants.question: Splenomegaly is enlargement of which organ?

visualDescription: SPLEN/O connecting to spleen illustration at upper left abdomen

quizQuestion: The combining form cyst/o refers to which organ?
explanation: Cyst/o comes from Greek 'kystis' meaning bladder or sac. Found in cystitis (bladder inflammation), cystoscopy (bladder camera exam), and cystectomy (bladder removal).
wowFactor: A cystoscope is a camera inserted into the bladder through the urethra — cyst/o from Greek 'kystis' means sac or bladder!

variants.question: Cystitis is inflammation of which organ?

visualDescription: CYST/O connecting to urinary bladder anatomy illustration

quizQuestion: The combining form urethr/o refers to which organ?
explanation: Urethr/o comes from Greek 'urethra' meaning the canal for urine from bladder to outside the body. Found in urethritis (urethra inflammation), urethroplasty (urethra repair), and urethroscopy.
wowFactor: The urethra and ureter are often confused — urethr/o is the EXIT tube from the bladder; ureter/o carries urine from kidney to bladder!

variants.question: Urethritis is inflammation of which structure?

visualDescription: URETHR/O connecting to urethra exiting the bladder illustration

quizQuestion: The combining form ureter/o refers to which organ?
explanation: Ureter/o refers to the ureter, the tube carrying urine from the kidney to the bladder. Found in ureteritis (ureter inflammation), ureteroplasty (ureter repair), and ureterolithiasis (kidney stones in the ureter).
wowFactor: Kidney stones that enter the ureter cause extreme pain — ureter/o is the tube from kidney to bladder, distinct from the urethra (bladder to outside)!

variants.question: The ureter connects the kidney to which organ?

visualDescription: URETER/O connecting to ureter tube between kidney and bladder

quizQuestion: The combining form hyster/o refers to which organ?
explanation: Hyster/o comes from Greek 'hystera' meaning uterus or womb. Found in hysterectomy (uterus removal), hysteroscopy (uterus camera exam), and hysteria (historically and incorrectly attributed to the uterus).
wowFactor: The word 'hysteria' was once blamed on the uterus by ancient Greeks — hyster/o from Greek 'hystera' meaning womb!

variants.question: A hysterectomy is surgical removal of which organ?

visualDescription: HYSTER/O connecting to anatomical uterus illustration

quizQuestion: The combining form oophor/o refers to which organ?
explanation: Oophor/o comes from Greek 'oophoron' meaning egg bearer (oon = egg, phoros = bearing). Found in oophorectomy (ovary removal), oophoritis (ovary inflammation), and oophoropexy.
wowFactor: Oophor/o literally means 'egg bearer' in Greek — oon (egg) + phoros (bearer) — because the ovaries produce and release eggs!

variants.question: Oophoritis is inflammation of which organ?

visualDescription: OOPHOR/O connecting to ovary illustration with follicles

quizQuestion: The combining form mast/o refers to which organ?
explanation: Mast/o comes from Greek 'mastos' meaning breast. Found in mastitis (breast inflammation), mastectomy (breast removal), and gynecomastia (enlarged breast tissue in males).
wowFactor: Mastodons were named for their breast-shaped tooth cusps — mast/o from Greek 'mastos' means breast, the same root as mastectomy!

variants.question: Mastitis is inflammation of which organ?

visualDescription: MAST/O connecting to breast anatomy cross-section illustration

quizQuestion: The combining form orchi/o refers to which organ?
explanation: Orchi/o comes from Greek 'orchis' meaning testis. Found in orchitis (testis inflammation), orchidectomy (testis removal), and cryptorchidism (undescended testis).
wowFactor: Orchids were named after the testis — orchi/o from Greek 'orchis' — because their tubers resemble the shape of testes!

variants.question: Orchitis is inflammation of which organ?

visualDescription: ORCHI/O connecting to testis anatomy illustration

quizQuestion: The combining form prostat/o refers to which organ?
explanation: Prostat/o comes from Greek 'prostates' meaning one who stands before (it stands before the bladder). Found in prostatitis (prostate inflammation), prostatectomy (prostate removal), and prostate-specific antigen (PSA).
wowFactor: The prostate literally 'stands before' the bladder — prostat/o from Greek 'prostates' meaning 'one who stands in front'!

variants.question: Prostatitis is inflammation of which gland?

visualDescription: PROSTAT/O connecting to prostate gland anatomy below the bladder

quizQuestion: The combining form thyr/o refers to which organ?
explanation: Thyr/o comes from Greek 'thyreos' meaning shield (the thyroid cartilage resembles a shield). Found in thyroiditis (thyroid inflammation), thyroidectomy (thyroid removal), and hypothyroidism.
wowFactor: The thyroid is shaped like a butterfly and controls your metabolism — thyr/o from Greek 'thyreos' means 'shield' for its protective cartilage!

variants.question: Hypothyroidism is underactivity of which gland?

visualDescription: THYR/O connecting to butterfly-shaped thyroid gland at the throat

quizQuestion: The combining form adren/o refers to which organ?
explanation: Adren/o comes from Latin 'ad' (near) + 'renes' (kidneys) — the adrenal glands sit atop the kidneys. Found in adrenaline (epinephrine), adrenalitis, and adrenal cortex/medulla.
statement: Adren/o refers to the adrenal gland.
wowFactor: Adrenaline — the fight-or-flight hormone — is named for the adrenal glands: adren/o from Latin meaning 'near the kidney'!

variants.question: Adrenaline is secreted by which gland?

visualDescription: ADREN/O connecting to adrenal gland sitting atop kidney illustration

quizQuestion: The combining form aden/o refers to which organ?
explanation: Aden/o comes from Greek 'aden' meaning gland. Found in adenitis (gland inflammation), adenoma (gland tumor), adenopathy (gland disease), and adenocarcinoma (gland cancer).
wowFactor: Adenoids (the lymphoid tissue in your throat) literally means 'gland-like' — aden/o from Greek 'aden' means gland!

variants.question: An adenoma is a tumor originating in which tissue?

visualDescription: ADEN/O connecting to generic gland cross-section with secretory cells

quizQuestion: The combining form angi/o refers to which organ?
explanation: Angi/o comes from Greek 'angeion' meaning vessel or container. Found in angiography (blood vessel imaging), angioplasty (vessel repair), and angiogenesis (new vessel growth).
statement: Angi/o refers to a blood vessel.
wowFactor: Tumor angiogenesis — when cancers grow their own blood supply — is a key target for cancer drugs: angi/o means 'vessel' in Greek!

variants.question: Angiography is an imaging technique for which structure?

visualDescription: ANGI/O connecting to branching blood vessel network illustration

quizQuestion: The combining form arteri/o refers to which organ?
explanation: Arteri/o comes from Greek 'arteria' meaning airway or artery (ancient Greeks thought arteries carried air). Found in arteriosclerosis (artery hardening), arteriography (artery imaging), and arteritis.
wowFactor: Ancient Greeks named arteries 'arteriai' thinking they carried air — the word arteria meant 'air carrier' before it was corrected!

variants.question: Arteriosclerosis is hardening of which structure?

visualDescription: ARTERI/O connecting to cross-section of artery with thick muscular wall

quizQuestion: The combining form phleb/o refers to which organ?
explanation: Phleb/o comes from Greek 'phleps/phlebos' meaning vein. Found in phlebotomy (blood drawing from a vein), phlebitis (vein inflammation), and phlebology (vein study).
wowFactor: Phlebotomy — drawing blood — literally means 'cutting a vein': phleb/o (vein) + tomy (cutting). It's one of the oldest medical procedures!

variants.question: Phlebitis is inflammation of which structure?

visualDescription: PHLEB/O connecting to vein cross-section with one-way valve visible

quizQuestion: The combining form hemat/o refers to which organ?
explanation: Hemat/o comes from Greek 'haima/haimatos' meaning blood. Found in hematology (blood study), hematoma (blood pooling), hematuria (blood in urine), and hematocrit (blood cell percentage).
wowFactor: A hematoma is a pocket of blood — hemat/o from Greek 'haima' meaning blood, the same root as hemoglobin and hemorrhage!

variants.question: A hematologist specializes in the study of which substance?

visualDescription: HEMAT/O connecting to red blood cells under microscope illustration

quizQuestion: The combining form chondr/o refers to which organ?
explanation: Chondr/o comes from Greek 'chondros' meaning cartilage or grain. Found in chondritis (cartilage inflammation), chondroma (cartilage tumor), and chondrocyte (cartilage cell).
wowFactor: Chondrichthyes — sharks and rays — are named for their cartilage skeletons: chondr/o (cartilage) + ichthys (fish)!

variants.question: Chondritis is inflammation of which tissue?

visualDescription: CHONDR/O connecting to hyaline cartilage cross-section showing chondrocytes

quizQuestion: The combining form tend/o refers to which organ?
explanation: Tend/o comes from Latin 'tendo' meaning to stretch (tendons stretch from muscle to bone). Found in tendinitis (tendon inflammation), tendinopathy (tendon disease), and tenotomy (tendon cutting).
wowFactor: The Achilles tendon — the thickest tendon in your body — connects your calf muscle to your heel bone, a classic tend/o structure!

variants.question: Tendinitis is inflammation of which structure?

visualDescription: TEND/O connecting to tendon attaching muscle to bone illustration

quizQuestion: The combining form crani/o refers to which organ?
explanation: Crani/o comes from Greek 'kranion' meaning skull or head. Found in craniotomy (skull opening surgery), craniology (skull study), and intracranial (inside the skull).
wowFactor: A craniotomy — opening the skull for brain surgery — is named crani/o (skull) + tomy (cutting): one of the most dramatic surgical procedures!

variants.question: Intracranial pressure refers to pressure inside which structure?

visualDescription: CRANI/O connecting to skull anatomy illustration showing cranial bones

quizQuestion: The combining form vertebr/o refers to which organ?
explanation: Vertebr/o comes from Latin 'vertebra' meaning joint or bone of the spine (from 'vertere' meaning to turn). Found in vertebral column, vertebroplasty (vertebra repair), and vertebral fracture.
wowFactor: There are 33 vertebrae in the human spine — vertebr/o from Latin 'vertere' (to turn) because the spine allows rotation!

variants.question: Vertebroplasty is a procedure to repair which structure?

visualDescription: VERTEBR/O connecting to spinal column illustration with individual vertebra highlighted

quizQuestion: The combining form myel/o refers to which organ?
explanation: Myel/o comes from Greek 'myelos' meaning marrow or spinal cord. Found in myelitis (spinal cord inflammation), myelogram (spinal cord imaging), and myelopathy (spinal cord disease). Note: it also refers to bone marrow.
statement: Myel/o refers to the spinal cord.
wowFactor: Myel/o is a tricky root — it means BOTH spinal cord AND bone marrow: myelitis is spinal cord inflammation, but myeloma is a bone marrow cancer!

variants.question: Myelitis is inflammation of which structure?

visualDescription: MYEL/O connecting to spinal cord cross-section inside vertebral column

quizQuestion: The combining form retin/o refers to which organ?
explanation: Retin/o comes from Latin 'rete' meaning net (the retina looks like a fine net of photoreceptor cells). Found in retinitis (retina inflammation), retinopathy (retina disease), and retinal detachment.
wowFactor: Diabetic retinopathy is damage to the light-sensing retina — retin/o from Latin 'rete' (net) for its web-like photoreceptor structure!

variants.question: Retinopathy is disease affecting which structure of the eye?

visualDescription: RETIN/O connecting to retina at back of eye cross-section
